steps.title: Identify the signal that would test the claim
steps.content: The researchers claim awe inspires altruistic behavior. The study tests this with an awe condition (tall trees) and a control (nondescript building), measuring whether participants help pick up spilled pens. The key signal is whether the awe-exposed group shows more helping.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
Choice A (‘Participants who had been looking at the trees helped the experimenter pick up significantly more pens than did participants who had been looking at the building.’) shows the predicted pattern: tree-gazers helped significantly more, directly confirming that awe prompted altruism. This is correct.

An incorrect response measures the language participants used in a postexperiment survey. That tells us how they described their feelings, not whether awe caused the helping behavior — it confuses the direction of the question.

Choice C (‘Participants who did not help the experimenter pick up the pens were significantly more likely to report having experienced a feeling of awe, regardless of whether they looked at the building or the trees.’) shows that non-helpers reported awe regardless of condition. This would actually contradict the claim: if people who felt awe still did not help, awe is not reliably linked to altruism.

An incorrect response measures whether participants noticed the dropped pens. Noticing is a matter of attention, not altruism — it does not show that awe increased helping.

That confirms A is correct.
